197	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
198	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
199	
200	\begin{theorem}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
204	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
206	  
207	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
208	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
209	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
210	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
212	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
213	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
214	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
215	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
216	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
217	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
218	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
221	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
222	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
223	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
224	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
225	  %
226	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
227	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
228	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
229	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
230	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
231	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
232	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
233	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
234	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
235	  %
236	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
237	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
238	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
239	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
240	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
242	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
243	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
244	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
245	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
246	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
247	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
248	      finite.
249	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
250	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
251	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
252	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
253	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
254	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
255	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
256	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
257	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
258	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
259	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
260	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
261	  \end{enumerate}
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
265	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
266	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
267	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
268	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
269	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
270	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
271	strata for different twists.
272	
273	\begin{lemma}
274	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
275	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
276	  
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
278	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
279	  % L1849-L1885).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let \(n=0\), so that \(\PP^n_S=S\).
282	  %  For any \(s\in S\), the \textbf{fiber} \(\F|_s\) of \(\F\) over \(s\)
283	  %  will mean the pull-back of \(\F\) to the subscheme
284	  %  \(\spec \kappa(s)\) [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
285	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to the fiber over \(s\) is the degree \(0\)
286	  %  polynomial \(e \in \Q[\lambda]\), where \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s\).
287	  %
288	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of \(\F|_s\) prolongs to a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\)
289	  %  to give a set of generators for \(F|_U\). Repeating this argument,
290	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in which
291	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
292	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
293	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
294	  %  Let \(I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V\) be the ideal sheaf
295	  %  formed by the entries of the \(e\times m\) matrix
296	  %  \((\psi_{i,j})\) [...].
297	  %  Let \(V_e\) be the closed subscheme of \(V\) defined by \(I_e\).
298	  %  For any morphism of schemes
299	  %  \(f: T\to V\), the pull-back sequence [...]
300	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
301	  %  Hence the pull-back \(f^*\F\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module of
302	  %  rank \(e\) if and only if \(f^*\psi =0\), that is,
303	  %  \(f\) factors via the subscheme \(V_e\hra V\) defined by the vanishing of all
304	  %  entries \(\psi_{i,j}\).
305	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
306	  %
307	  %  As the rank of the matrix \((\psi_{i,j})\) is lower semi-continuous,
308	  %  it follows that the function \(e\) is upper semi-continuous, which
309	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
310	  %  when \(n=0\)."
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case \(n=0\) of the main theorem).}
312	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(\F\) be a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
313	  For each integer \(e \ge 0\), there exists a locally-closed subscheme
314	  \(S_e \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set is
315	  \(\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}\). The family \(\{S_e\}_e\) is a
316	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of \(S\),
317	  and a morphism \(f : T \to S\) pulls back \(\F\) to a locally-free
318	  \(\OO_T\)-module of rank \(e\) if and only if \(f\) factors through \(S_e\).  The
319	  function \(s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\) is upper-semicontinuous on \(S\).
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  
324	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for \(s \in S\), Nakayama produces a
325	  neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a right-exact local presentation
326	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0\) where
327	  \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).  The closed subscheme \(V_e \hookrightarrow V\)
328	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of \(\psi\)
329	  represents the locus where \(\F\) becomes locally free of rank \(e\): by
330	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change \(f : T \to V\) has
331	  \(f^* \F\) locally free of rank \(e\) iff \(f^* \psi = 0\), iff \(f\) factors
332	  through \(V_e\).  Glueing the \(V_e\) over the cover of \(S\) by such
333	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with \(V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}\)
334	  to get the strictly-equal-to-\(e\) stratum) produces the locally-closed
335	  \(S_e\). Upper-semicontinuity of \(e\) follows because the rank of \(\psi\) is
336	  lower-semicontinuous.
337	\end{proof}

339	\begin{lemma}
340	[Noetherian induction reduction to the flat case]
341	  \label{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', General case
344	  % opening paragraphs (read from
345	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1888-L1929).
346	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
347	  % "\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer \(n\) to be arbitrary.
348	  %  The idea of the proof is as follows:
349	  %  We show the existence of a stratification
350	  %  of \(S\) which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
351	  %  stratifications for direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N\) for some
352	  %  integer \(N\) [...].
353	  %
354	  %  As \(S\) is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
355	  %  and these are closed in \(S\).
356	  %  Let \(Y\) be an irreducible component of \(S\), and let \(U\) be the
357	  %  non-empty open subset of \(Y\) which consists of all points which do not
358	  %  lie on any other irreducible component of \(S\).
359	  %  Let \(U\) be given the reduced subscheme
360	  %  structure. Note that this makes \(U\) an integral scheme, which is a
361	  %  locally closed subscheme of \(S\).
362	  %  By Theorem REF on generic flatness,
363	  %  \(U\) has a non-empty open subscheme \(V\) such that
364	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_V\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_V\).
365	  %  Now repeating the argument with \(S\) replaced by its reduced
366	  %  closed subscheme \(S-V\),
367	  %  it follows by noetherian induction on \(S\) that there exist
368	  %  finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
369	  %  \(V_i\) of \(S\) such that set-theoretically
370	  %  \(|S|\) is the union of the \(|V_i|\) and
371	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_{V_i}\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_{V_i}\).
372	  %  As each \(V_i\) is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
373	  %  are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows
374	  %  that only finitely many polynomials occur in \(V_i\)
375	  %  in the family of Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\)
376	  %  as \(s\) varies over points of \(V_i\). This allows us to conclude
377	  %  the following:
378	  %
379	  %  \textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials
380	  %  \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\) occur, as \(s\)
381	  %  varies over all of \(S\)."
382	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (general case, reduction by Noetherian induction).}
383	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) the relative projective
384	  space, and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on \(\PP^n_S\).  Then there exist finitely many
385	  reduced, locally-closed, pairwise disjoint subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\)
386	  whose set-theoretic union is \(|S|\) and such that \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is
387	  flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) for each \(i\). Consequently, only finitely many
388	  Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) occur as \(s\) varies
389	  over \(S\).
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  
394	  By [Nitsure], \S4: \(S\) being noetherian, decompose \(|S| = \bigcup Y_j\) into
395	  finitely many irreducible components, each closed.  For an irreducible
396	  component \(Y\), let \(U \subseteq Y\) be the open subset not lying on any other
397	  component, given the reduced subscheme structure (an integral, locally-closed
398	  subscheme of \(S\)).  By Theorem~REF applied to
399	  \(\F|_{\PP^n_U}\), there is a non-empty open \(V \subseteq U\) over which \(\F\) is
400	  flat.  Replace \(S\) by the reduced closed complement \(S \setminus V\) and
401	  repeat. Noetherianity of \(S\) terminates the induction in finitely many
402	  steps, producing the \(V_i\).  On each \(V_i\) the Hilbert polynomial is locally
403	  constant (flat families have locally constant Hilbert polynomial), hence
404	  takes finitely many values on the noetherian (hence quasi-compact) scheme
405	  \(V_i\); the finite union of finite sets is finite.
406	\end{proof}

408	\begin{lemma}
409	[Assembly of the strata by Noetherian induction]
410	  \label{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
411	  
412	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Assembly via
413	  % direct images \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\) (read from
414	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1992-L2090).
415	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
416	  % "Let \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) denote the projection. Consider the
417	  %  coherent sheaves \(E_0, \ldots, E_n\) on \(S\), defined by
418	  %  \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.\)
419	  %  By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
420	  %  \(n\) of \(\PP^n_S\) is \(0\)) to the sheaf \(E_0\) on \(\PP^0_S=S\), we get
421	  %  a stratification \((W_{e_0})\) of \(S\)
422	  %  indexed by integers \(e_0\), such that for any morphism

424	  %  of rank \(e_0\) if and only if \(f\) factors via \(W_{e_0} \hra S\).

426	  %  flattening stratification \((W_{e_0,e_1})\) for \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\), and so on.

428	  %  locally closed subschemes

431	  %  \(f^*E_i\) for \(i=0,\ldots,n\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-modules of
432	  %  of constant rank \(e_i\) if and only if \(f\) factors
433	  %  via \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \hra S\). [...]
434	  %  This shows that at any point \(s\in W_f\), the Hilbert polynomial
435	  %  \(P_s(m)\) equals \(f\). The desired locally closed subscheme \(S_f \subset S\),
436	  %  whose existence is asserted by the theorem,
437	  %  will turn out to be a certain closed subscheme \(S_f \subset W_f\) whose
438	  %  underlying subset is all of \(|W_f|\). [...]
439	  %  As \(S_f\) is the flattening stratification for \(\pi_*\F(p)\),
440	  %  the property (iii) of the theorem follows from the corresponding property in
441	  %  the case \(n=0\), applied to the sheaf \(\pi_*\F(p)\) on \(S\)."
442	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (assembly step combining the \(n=0\) special case
443	  applied to direct images \(\pi_*\F(N+i)\)).}
444	  Let \(S\), \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\), \(\F\) be as in
445	  Theorem~REF.  There exists an integer
446	  \(N \ge 0\) such that for every \(s \in S\) the higher cohomologies
447	  \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) vanish for all \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N\), and the base-change
448	  homomorphism \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism
449	  for \(m \ge N\), \(s \in S\).  Iterating the \(n=0\) stratification
450	  (Lemma~REF) on the coherent sheaves \(E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)\)
451	  for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) produces a finite collection of locally-closed
452	  subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\) indexed by tuples
453	  \((e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}\). Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
454	  numerical polynomial \(f \in \Q[\lambda]\) of degree \(\le n\) with
455	  \(f(N+i) = e_i\) gives a finite stratification \(\{W_f\}\) of \(S\), and the
456	  locally-closed subschemes \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) of
457	  Theorem~REF are obtained by further

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

162	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}

197	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
198	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
199	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),

209	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
210	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).

400	  the functor \(F\) sending an \(X\)-scheme \(h : S \to X\) to the set of
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra maps \(h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\). A morphism

438	  %  \(\)

447	  %  \longrightarrow

453	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
454	  The construction \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) assembles into
455	  a contravariant functor
456	  \[
457	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
458	  \]
459	  from quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras to affine \(X\)-schemes: a morphism of
460	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) induces, by the universal
461	  property (Theorem~REF) applied to the pair
462	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
463	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
464	  an \(X\)-morphism

479	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))\( defines an \)X$-morphism
480	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) via the
481	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
482	  representing bijection in \(\mathcal{A}\). For the equivalence claim, \(\underline{\Spec}_X\)
483	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
484	  \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
485	  (locally identifying with \(\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)\)
486	  via Theorem~REF) reconstructs \(\mathcal{A}\) from

490	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
491	  \(\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)\)).
492	\end{proof}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}

252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}

322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}

402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

2996	  \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin poset and all the relevant squares commute by
2997	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); but the module-structure transport (c) does
2998	  \emph{not} trivialise --- it is the substantive content, the place where the two sides
2999	  differ by a real ring isomorphism rather than an identity. Assembling the sectionwise
3000	  isomorphisms into a presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism, and sheafifying through the
3001	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, yields the stated isomorphism
3002	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
3003	\end{proof}
3004	
3005	\begin{lemma}
3006	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
3007	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
3008	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
3009	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
3010	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3011	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
3012	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
3013	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
3014	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3015	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
3016	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
3017	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
3018	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
3019	  % is an isomorphism."
3020	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
3021	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
3022	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
3023	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
3024	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
3025	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
3026	\end{lemma}
3027	
3028	\begin{proof}
3029	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso,
3030	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3031	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
3032	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
3033	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
3034	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
3035	  \[
3036	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
3037	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
3038	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
3039	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
3040	  \]
3041	  \begin{enumerate}
3042	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
3043	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
3044	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
3045	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
3046	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
3047	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
3048	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
3049	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
3050	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
3051	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
3052	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
3053	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\(\mathtt{dual\_unit\_iso}\)):
3054	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
3055	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
3056	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
3057	  \end{enumerate}
3058	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
3059	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
3060	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
3061	\end{proof}
3062	
3063	\begin{lemma}
3064	
3065	\leanok
3066	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
3067	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
3068	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
3069	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
3070	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
3071	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
3072	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
3073	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
3074	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
3075	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
3076	  isomorphism.
3077	\end{lemma}
3078	
3079	\begin{proof}
3080	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
3081	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
3082	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
3083	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
3084	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
3085	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
3086	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
3087	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
3088	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
3089	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
3090	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
3091	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
3092	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
3093	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
3094	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
3095	  itself is an isomorphism.
3096	\end{proof}
3097	
3098	\begin{definition}
3099	
3100	
3101	\leanok
3102	[Scheme-level linearity wrapper \(\mathtt{homMk}\) (A-bridge step (ii))]
3103	  \label{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
3105	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism
3106	  \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf} \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of
3107	  the underlying presheaves of abelian groups, together with a hypothesis \(hg\) that
3108	  \(g\) is \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear sectionwise, \(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg\) is the
3109	  morphism \(M \to N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) that this data determines. It
3110	  is a thin wrapper that packages \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.homMk}\) at the scheme
3111	  level: the sectionwise-linearity datum promotes the ab-group morphism to a genuine
3112	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
3113	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\
3114	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,X).\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg) = g\). This is step
3115	  (ii) of the A-bridge \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}: once the
3116	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism has been glued, \(\mathtt{homMk}\) re-attaches the
3117	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity to produce the global morphism of modules.
3118	\end{definition}
3119	
3120	\begin{lemma}
3121	[Compatible local morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global morphism]
3122	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
3123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
3124	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
3125	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
3126	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
3127	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
3128	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3129	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
3130	\end{lemma}
3131	
3132	\begin{proof}
3133	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3134	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
3135	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
3136	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
3137	
3138	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
3139	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
3140	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
3141	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
3142	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
3143	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
3144	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
3145	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
3146	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
3147	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
3148	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
3149	  strictly more bookkeeping.
3150	
3151	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
3152	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
3153	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
3154	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
3155	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
3156	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
3157	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
3158	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
3159	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
3160	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
3161	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
3162	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
3163	
3164	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
3165	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
3166	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
3167	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
3168	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
3169	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
3170	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
3171	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
3172	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The dual restriction-iso
3173	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) consumes the \emph{functor-direction} face of the
3174	  same equivalence; that the two share \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} as a
3175	  common root is why the shared bridge, once built, unblocks both at once.
3176	
3177	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
3178	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
3179	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
3180	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
3181	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
3182	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
3183	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
3184	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
3185	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
3186	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
3187	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
3188	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
3189	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
3190	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
3191	
3192	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
3193	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
3194	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
3195	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
3196	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
3197	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3198	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3199	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
3200	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
3201	
3202	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
3203	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
3204	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
3205	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
3206	  building that shared bridge, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
3207	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
3208	  naturality, built as a standalone axiom-clean lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
3209	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
3210	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the abandoned tensor-stalk commutation.
3211	\end{proof}
3212	
3213	\begin{remark}
3214	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
3215	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
3216	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
3217	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
3218	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
3219	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
3220	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
3221	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
3222	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
3223	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
3224	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
3225	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
3226	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
3227	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
3228	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
3229	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
3230	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
3231	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
3232	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
3233	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
3234	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
3235	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
3236	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
3237	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
3238	
3239	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
3240	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
3241	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
3242	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
3243	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
3244	\end{remark}
3245	
3246	\begin{remark}
3247	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
3248	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
3249	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
3250	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
3251	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
3252	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
3253	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
3254	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
3255	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
3256	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
3257	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
3258	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
3259	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
3260	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
3261	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
3262	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Cross01 Substrate}
2	\label{chap:Genus0BaseObjects_Cross01Substrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Cross01Substrate.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke
6	% substrate file landing two reusable lemmas needed to close the Lane B
7	% \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-scaling cocycle (path (III.c) of
8	% chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity) together with two off-target sorries
9	% (\texttt{gm\_geomIrred}, \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}) in
10	% \texttt{Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean}. Both substrates are
11	% generic in the geometric situation (the closed-immersion lift lemma is
12	% scheme-level; the tensor-domain lemma is ring-level keyed on the
13	% \texttt{projectiveLineBar} grading) and live in their own file rather
14	% than inline so they can be re-used by the three GmScaling
15	% applications and, if upstreamed, by Mathlib itself.
16	%
17	% The recipe consolidated below was produced by the
18	% \texttt{mathlib-analogist} subagent in iter-189 and is preserved
19	% verbatim in \texttt{analogies/lane-b-substrate.md} (Section
20	% ``Concrete project-side recipe''). The chapter itself is the
21	% prover-facing specification.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}\leanok
61	[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
62	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
64	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
65	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
66	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
67	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
68	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
69	  \(i\):
70	  \[
71	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
72	    \;\iff\;
73	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
74	  \]
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{proof}
77	
78	
79	\leanok
80	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
81	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
82	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
83	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
84	
85	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
86	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
87	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
88	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
89	
90	  \begin{enumerate}
91	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
92	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
93	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
94	    ideal sheaf, so
95	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
96	    (Mathlib:
97	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
98	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
99	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
100	    The unconditional inclusion
101	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
102	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
103	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
104	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
105	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
106	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
107	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
108	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
109	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
110	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
111	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
112	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
114	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
115	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
116	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
117	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
118	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
119	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
120	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
121	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
122	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
123	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
124	    \[
125	      i.\mathrm{ker}
126	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
128	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
129	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
130	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
131	    \]
132	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
133	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
134	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
135	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
136	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
137	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
138	  \end{enumerate}
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
142	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
146	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
148	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
149	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
150	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
151	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
152	  tensor product
153	  \[
154	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
155	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
156	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
157	  \]
158	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
159	\end{theorem}
160	\begin{proof}
161	
162	\leanok
163	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
164	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
165	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
166	  preserve the domain property.
167	
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
170	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
171	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
172	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
173	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
174	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
175	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
176	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
177	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
178	    threading.)
179	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
180	    Recalling
181	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
182	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
183	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
184	    \[
185	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
186	      \;\cong\;
187	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
188	    \]
189	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
190	    isomorphism.
191	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
192	    Tensoring with the localisation step
193	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
194	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
195	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
196	    isomorphism
197	    \[
198	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
199	      \;\cong\;
200	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
201	    \]
202	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
203	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
204	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
205	    tensored polynomial ring.
206	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
207	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
208	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
209	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
210	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
211	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
212	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
213	    localisation is then a domain by
214	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
215	  \end{enumerate}
216	
217	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
218	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
219	\end{proof}
220
