3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced
106	% by the iter-187 refactor-`ocofp-steps3to5` dispatch. The substantive
107	% mathematical content is in the Definition block below; these helper
108	% blocks pin the substrate hooks the prover-side construction uses.
109	
110	\begin{definition}
111	\leanok
112	[Presheaf carrier for the line bundle of a closed point]
113	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf}
115	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
116	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
117	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
118	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
119	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
120	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
121	  \[
122	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
128	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
129	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
130	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{lemma}
134	\leanok
135	[Sheaf property of the carrier presheaf]
136	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
138	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf,
139	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
140	  The presheaf \texttt{carrierPresheaf}\(_P\) (defined via the
141	  carrier-refinement of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf})
142	  is a sheaf in the appropriate Grothendieck topology on \(C\). Concretely,
143	  the locality + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections
144	  reduce (via \(\texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_forget}\)) to the sheaf
145	  condition on the underlying Set-valued functor: each
146	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U)\) is a subset of the constant
147	  \(K(C)\)-sheaf restricted to \(U\), and the sheaf property in the
148	  constant-sheaf base case is direct. The project's
149	  \texttt{toModuleKSheaf} template
150	  (\texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean}) is
151	  the model for this argument.
152	\end{lemma}
153	
154	\begin{definition}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]
175	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
177	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
178	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
179	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
180	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
181	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
182	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
183	  helper.
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}
268	
269	\begin{definition}
270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
295	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
296	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
297	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
298	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
299	  \[
300	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
301	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
302	  \]
303	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
304	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
305	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
307	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
308	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
309	
310	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
312	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
313	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
314	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
315	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
316	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
317	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
318	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
319	  and its body is gated on the body of
320	  REF.
321	\end{definition}

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\section{Immediate corollaries}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.

69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same

76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}

153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
159	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
163	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
164	  % topological spaces."
165	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
166	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
173	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
178	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.
182

242	  % prover-side construction). The classical reference is Hartshorne

245	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate (Hartshorne~II.1,
246	  Exercise~1.16(c) packaged with the sections-surjectivity input from
247	  II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) as a hypothesis).}
248	  Let $X$ be a topological space and let
249	  \[
250	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
251	  \]
252	  be a short exact sequence in
253	  $\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
254	  (\Module \bar k)$. Suppose:
255	  \begin{enumerate}
256	    \item $\mathcal F_2$ is flasque (in the sense of

271	  push $\tilde T$ through the map $\mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3$ at
272	  $U$. Naturality of the morphism on opens identifies the restriction

276	\begin{lemma}
277	
278	\leanok
279	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
280	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
282	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
283	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
284	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
285	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
286	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
287	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
288	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
289	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
290	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
291	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
292	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
293	  let
294	  \[
295	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
296	  \]
297	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
298	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
299	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
300	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
301	
302	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
303	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
304	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
305	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
306	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
307	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
308	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
309	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
310	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{lemma}\leanok
314	[Injective objects of $\mathrm{Sheaf}(X, \Module \bar k)$ are flasque]
315	  \label{lem:isFlasque_injective}
316	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque}
317	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
318	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Lemma~2.4 (read from
319	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf — the project's local
320	  % .md transcript does not extend to this passage, so no verbatim quote
321	  % is pasted in this block per the iter-197 directive; the citation
322	  % pointer is recorded for downstream retrieval).
323	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Lemma~2.4.}
324	
325	  Let \(I\) be an injective object of
326	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
327	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then \(I\) is flasque in the sense of
328	  \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}: for every inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) of
329	  opens of \(X\), the restriction map
330	  \(I(U) \to I(V)\) is surjective on underlying \(\bar k\)-modules.
331	
332	  The classical argument (Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4) uses the
333	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\) functor: for an open inclusion
334	  \(j_V \colon V \hookrightarrow X\), one has the short exact sequence
335	  \(0 \to j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to j_{U!}\mathcal O_U \to
336	  j_{U!}\mathcal O_U / j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to 0\), and injectivity of
337	  \(I\) makes the induced
338	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(j_{U!}\mathcal O_U, I) \to
339	    \mathrm{Hom}(j_{V!}\mathcal O_V, I)\) surjective. Translating
340	  through the \(j_{(-)!} \dashv j_{(-)}^*\) adjunction (or the
341	  equivalent presheaf form for the constant ring sheaf \(\bar k\))
342	  identifies the two Hom-sets with \(I(U)\) and \(I(V)\) respectively,
343	  delivering the surjective restriction map.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
348	  % NOTE (iter-197 lean-vs-blueprint-checker J-2): Out-of-loop iter-196
349	  % directive — closure of this lemma is excluded from the Lane H
350	  % iter-196 scope per the progress-critic scope reduction. The

352	  % (the open-immersion pushforward-with-compact-support adjoint),
353	  % which Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` does not ship in the
354	  % sheaf-of-modules generality the lemma needs. Estimated closure
355	  % cost: ~100-150 LOC of project-side `j_!` scaffolding OR a Mathlib
356	  % upstream PR. The Lean file
357	  % `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean` carries this
358	  % declaration as a typed sorry at L613-618. This block is pinned
359	  % here so that `sync_leanok` and the lean-vs-blueprint-checker can
360	  % track its closure status independently of the headline theorem
361	  % below (which depends on it transitively via the private
362	  % auxiliary `HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux`).
363	  See the lemma statement above for the proof sketch; the formalisation
364	  is deferred to a future iteration after a Mathlib \(j_!\)
365	  construction is available.
366	\end{proof}

368	\begin{theorem}
369	
370	\leanok
371	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
372	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
374	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_injective,
375	        lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact,
376	        lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
377	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
378	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
380	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
381	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
382	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
383	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
384	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
385	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
386	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
387	  \[
388	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
389	  \]
390	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
391	\end{theorem}

2	\chapter{The rational-curve isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) (RR.4)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}
4	\uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections, lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso, cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero, thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero, def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
5	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
6	% + IV \S2, opening of "Hurwitz's Theorem" (degree of a finite morphism of
7	% curves is the function-field extension degree; read from PDF page 316)
8	% + I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field \(\Leftrightarrow\) smooth-projective-curve
