12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
14	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
26	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
28	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
44	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
45	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
46	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
47	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
48	\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with
56	a \(k\)-rational point \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' is a separate sub-build hosted
57	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
58	consumer) and is out of scope here.
59	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
60	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
61	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signature
62	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
63	project's standing curve typeclass package:
64	\begin{quote}
65	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
66	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
67	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
68	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
69	\end{quote}
70	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
71	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
72	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
73	\(g(C)\) is the one defined in \texttt{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
74	namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) via the project's
75	\(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
76	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
77	\(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is the project's
78	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of
79	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean}.
80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	\leanok
84	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
85	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
86	  
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
89	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
90	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
91	  \[
92	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
93	  \]
94	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
95	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
96	  \[
97	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
98	  \]
99	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
100	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
101	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
102	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
103	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
106	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
107	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
108	\end{lemma}
109	\begin{proof}
110	
111	
112	\leanok
113	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
114	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
115	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
116	  under the name
117	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
118	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
119	  \begin{quote}
120	  \small
121	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
122	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
123	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
124	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
125	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
127	  \end{quote}
128	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
129	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
130	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
131	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
132	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
133	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
134	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
135	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
136	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
137	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
138	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
139	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
140	  project-bespoke wrapper
141	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
142	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
143	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
144	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
145	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
146	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
147	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
148	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
149	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
150	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
151	\end{proof}
152	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
153	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
154	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
155	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
156	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
157	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
158	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
159	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
160	\begin{lemma}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
164	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
165	  
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
168	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
169	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
171	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
172	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
173	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
174	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
175	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
176	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
177	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
178	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
179	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
180	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
181	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
182	  \[
183	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
184	    \quad\text{and}\quad
185	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
186	  \]
187	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
188	  \[
189	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
190	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
191	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
192	  \]
193	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
194	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
195	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
196	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
197	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
198	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
199	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
200	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
201	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
202	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
203	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
204	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
205	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
206	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
207	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
208	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
209	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
210	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
211	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
212	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
213	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
214	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
215	\end{lemma}
216	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
217	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
218	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
219	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
220	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
221	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
222	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
223	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
224	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
225	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
226	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
227	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
228	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
229	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
230	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
231	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
232	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
233	%   2. New lemma
234	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
235	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
236	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
237	%      owed iter-191+).
238	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
239	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
240	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
241	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
242	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
243	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
244	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
245	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
246	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
247	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
248	\begin{proof}
249	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
250	  \leanok
251	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
252	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
253	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
254	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
255	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
256	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
257	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
258	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
259	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
260	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
261	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
262	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
263	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
264	  is finite.
265	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
266	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
267	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
268	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
269	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
270	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
271	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
272	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
273	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
274	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
275	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
276	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
277	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
278	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
279	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
280	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
281	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
282	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
283	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
284	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
285	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
286	\end{proof}

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),

8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173 introduced
100	    the \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} class
101	    (\ref{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}) bundling this conjunction;
102	    the order/principal lemmas downstream take this class as a typeclass
103	    hypothesis when they require the DVR structure at codim-1 points.
104	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
105	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
106	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
107	    \begin{quote}
108	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
109	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
111	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
112	    \end{quote}
113	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
114	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
115	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
116	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
117	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
118	    properness; integrality threaded).
119	\end{itemize}
120	\noindent
121	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
122	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
123	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
124	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
125	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
126	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
127	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
128	\begin{definition}
129	\leanok
130	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
131	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
133	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
134	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
135	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
137	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
138	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
139	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
140	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
141	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
142	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
143	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
144	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
146	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
147	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
148	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
149	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
150	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
152	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
153	  the Mathlib predicate
154	  \[
155	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
156	  \]
157	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
158	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
159	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
160	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
161	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
162	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
163	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
164	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
165	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
166	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
167	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
168	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
169	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
170	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
171	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
172	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
173	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
174	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
175	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
176	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
177	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
178	  \begin{itemize}
179	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
180	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
181	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
182	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
183	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
184	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
185	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
186	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
187	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
188	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
189	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
190	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
191	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
192	      uses.
193	  \end{itemize}
194	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
195	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
196	  needs.
197	\end{definition}
198	\begin{definition}
199	\leanok
200	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
201	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
202	  
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
204	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
205	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
206	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
207	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
209	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
210	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
211	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
212	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
214	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
215	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
216	  \[
217	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
218	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
219	  \]
220	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
221	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
222	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
223	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
226	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
227	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
228	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
229	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
230	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
231	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
232	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
233	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
234	\end{definition}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Regular in codimension one]
238	  \label{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne}
240	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II \S6, p.~130, the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)
241	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
242	  % Stacks pointers: tag 0BX5 (regular schemes), tag 02ME
243	  % (DVR-of-stalk characterisation at codim-1 points).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme
245	  % which is regular in codimension one."
246	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130 (the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)).}
247	  A scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in codimension one} when for every point
248	  \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) (equivalently,
249	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1\)) the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is
250	  a discrete valuation ring. The Lean encoding bundles this conjunction as
251	  a one-field \texttt{Prop} class so that downstream order / principal /
252	  degree lemmas may consume it as a typeclass hypothesis.
253	\end{definition}
254	\section{Open-immersion descent for prime divisors}
255	\label{sec:open_immersion_descent}
256	\textit{Source: Stacks Project tags 02IZ (stalk under open immersion) +
257	01J6 (open immersion preserves regularity); cross-referenced with the
258	project-local iter-183 substrate
259	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}.}
260	
261	The chapter's order / principal-divisor API is naturally stated on the
262	ambient scheme \(X\), but several downstream proofs (the
263	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) non-zero branch in
264	particular) require localising to an affine open \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
265	identifying prime divisors of \(X\) lying in \(U\) with prime divisors of
266	\(U\), and transporting stalk-level data along the open-immersion stalk
267	isomorphism. This section records the substrate bijection
268	\(\{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
269	 \;\simeq\; U.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\)
270	and the open-immersion descent of the \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
271	class.
272	
273	\begin{definition}
274	
275	\leanok
276	[Restriction of a prime divisor to an open subscheme]
277	  \label{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen}
279	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
280	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), a prime
281	  divisor \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), and a witness \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\),
282	  the restriction \(Y |_U : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages
283	  the point \(Y.\texttt{point}\) (viewed as a point of \(U\) via the open
284	  inclusion) with the coheight witness from
285	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) (project-local
286	  substrate, iter-183 \texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185).
287	\end{definition}
288	\begin{definition}
289	
290	\leanok
291	[Push of a prime divisor along an open immersion]
292	  \label{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen}
294	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
295	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), and a
296	  prime divisor \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), the push
297	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages the point
298	  \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U \hookrightarrow X\) (viewed as a point of \(X\))
299	  with the coheight witness from
300	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) applied in the
301	  opposite direction.
302	\end{definition}
303	\begin{lemma}
304	
305	\leanok
306	[Bijection of prime divisors under open immersion]
307	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_equivOpen}
308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen}
309	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen, def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
310	  For an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\) of a scheme \(X\), there is a
311	  bijection
312	  \[
313	    \{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
314	    \;\simeq\;
315	    U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}
316	  \]
317	  packaging the restriction (\ref{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}) and the
318	  push (\ref{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}) as mutual inverses. Both
319	  \texttt{left\_inv} and \texttt{right\_inv} hold definitionally
320	  (\texttt{rfl}) because both directions preserve the underlying point
321	  field via subtype eta + structure eta.
322	\end{lemma}
323	\begin{lemma}
324	
325	\leanok
326	[Stalk isomorphism across an open immersion]
327	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso}
329	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
330	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02IZ (stalk is invariant under restriction
331	  % to a non-empty open neighbourhood) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex).
332	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, tag 02IZ, plus Mathlib's
333	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}.}
334	  For \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\), the
335	  stalk presheaf of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) at the restricted point coincides,
336	  up to canonical isomorphism, with the stalk presheaf of \(X\) at
337	  \(Y.\texttt{point}\). The Lean wrapper packages Mathlib's
338	  \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\) for the project's
339	  \texttt{PrimeDivisor} bundle.
340	\end{lemma}
341	\begin{theorem}
342	
343	\leanok
344	[\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) descends along open immersion]
345	  \label{thm:isRegularInCodimensionOne_open}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen}
347	  \uses{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne, lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso,
348	    def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
350	  and \(U \hookrightarrow X\) an open subscheme with
351	  \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}\) (the latter is \emph{not}
352	  automatic from integrality of \(X\); it is threaded explicitly).
353	  Then \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) also satisfies
354	  \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\); the DVR-of-stalk property at a
355	  codim-1 point \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) is
356	  transported from the corresponding point
357	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) on the ambient
358	  scheme via the stalk isomorphism (\ref{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso})
359	  together with
360	  \(\texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing}\)
361	  on the ring-equiv image.
362	\end{theorem}
363	\paragraph{Lean-side note.} The 8 axiom-clean iter-200 substrate
364	declarations
365	(\(\texttt{PrimeDivisor.ext}\), \(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\),
366	\(\texttt{ofOpen}\), the two \(\texttt{@[simp]}\) field-extraction
367	helpers \(\texttt{restrictToOpen\_point}\) /
368	\(\texttt{ofOpen\_point}\), \(\texttt{equivOpen}\),
369	\(\texttt{stalkIso}\), and the descent instance
370	\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen}\)) consume the project-local

372	(\texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185) and Mathlib's
373	\(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\). Realised cost: \(+120\) LOC over
374	\texttt{WeilDivisor.lean} L153--L305 (iter-200).

378	  \label{def:functionFieldIso}
379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}
324	
325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.
385
